meta.name: Project Management Fundamentals
meta.description: Key project management concepts and terminology - methodologies, tools, roles, and best practices for successful project delivery.

cards.definition: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result. Has a defined beginning and end.
The application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet project requirements.
A group of related projects managed in a coordinated way to obtain benefits not available from managing them individually.
A collection of projects, programs, and operations managed together to achieve strategic objectives.
The series of phases a project passes through from start to finish. Can be predictive, agile, or hybrid.
A project approach where scope, schedule, and cost are determined early and changes are tightly controlled.
An iterative approach that delivers value in small increments, emphasizing collaboration, flexibility, and customer feedback.
Combines predictive and agile practices to fit project needs.
A document that formally authorizes a project and gives the project manager authority to apply resources.
Any individual or group who may affect, be affected by, or perceive themselves to be affected by the project.
cards.hint: Anyone with interest in the project.

cards.definition: A document listing stakeholders, their interests, influence, and engagement strategies.
cards.hint: Who they are + what they need.

cards.definition: The work required to deliver a product, service, or result with specified features and functions.
A hierarchical decomposition of project deliverables into smaller, manageable components.
The approved version of the project schedule used to measure performance.
The sequence of activities that determines the shortest possible project duration.
The approved version of the project budget used to measure cost performance.
Ensuring the project meets requirements through quality planning, assurance, and control.
An uncertain event that may have a positive or negative impact on project objectives.
A document listing identified risks, their analysis, and response plans.
Tracks current problems that require action, unlike risks which are uncertain.
A structured process for evaluating and approving changes to scope, schedule, or cost.
A group responsible for reviewing and approving project changes.
The person assigned to lead the project team and achieve project objectives.
Individuals performing project work, including specialists and contributors.
A leadership style common in agile where the leader supports the team by removing obstacles.
An agile framework using sprints, roles (Scrum Master, Product Owner, Developers), and ceremonies.
A fixed-length iteration (usually 1–4 weeks) where a Scrum team delivers a usable increment.
A prioritized list of features, enhancements, and fixes maintained by the Product Owner.
The set of items the team commits to completing during a sprint.
A short daily meeting where the team synchronizes work and identifies blockers.
A visual workflow method focused on limiting work in progress and improving flow.
The process of identifying what stakeholders need from the project.
Identifying business needs and recommending solutions that deliver value.
Short descriptions of functionality from the user's perspective, common in agile.
Conditions that must be met for a deliverable to be accepted.
The approved scope, schedule, and cost plan used to measure performance.
A technique that integrates scope, schedule, and cost to measure project performance.
A measure of cost performance: CV = EV – AC.
A measure of schedule performance: SV = EV – PV.
Acquiring goods or services from outside the organization.
Common types include fixed-price, cost-reimbursable, and time-and-materials.
Ensuring timely and appropriate planning, creation, distribution, and storage of project information.
A responsibility assignment chart showing who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed.
Building team skills, trust, and collaboration to improve performance.
Techniques include collaborating, compromising, smoothing, forcing, and withdrawing.
Documenting what went well and what could improve for future projects.
Finalizing all activities, obtaining acceptance, releasing resources, and archiving documents.
Ensuring the project delivers the expected business value after completion.
Internal assets such as templates, policies, and historical information that influence project success.
External or internal factors that influence project execution, such as culture, regulations, or market conditions.
The framework that defines roles, responsibilities, and decision-making processes for project oversight.
cards.hint: Who decides what in the project.

cards.definition: The overall structure of policies, procedures, and norms that guide organizational behavior and project alignment.
Common structures include functional, matrix (weak/strong), and projectized - each affecting authority and resource availability.
An organization where employees report to functional managers; project managers have little authority.
An organization where project managers have full authority and teams are dedicated to projects.
A structure where employees report to both functional and project managers. Can be weak, balanced, or strong.
A group that standardizes project processes and provides governance. Types: supportive, controlling, directive.
Provides templates and best practices; low control.
Requires compliance with project standards and methodologies.
Directly manages projects and assigns project managers.
A detailed description of project deliverables, boundaries, acceptance criteria, and assumptions.
Links requirements to objectives, deliverables, and test cases to ensure coverage.
Breaking down deliverables into smaller components for planning and estimating.
A detailed list of all activities required to complete project work.
Determining the logical order of activities using dependencies.
Finish-to-Start (FS), Start-to-Start (SS), Finish-to-Finish (FF), Start-to-Finish (SF).
Lead allows acceleration; lag introduces delay between activities.
Uses optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely estimates to calculate expected duration or cost.
Expected duration = (O + 4M + P) / 6.
Adjusting the schedule to address resource over-allocation.
Adjusting activities within float to avoid resource peaks without changing the critical path.
Developing an approximation of the cost of resources needed to complete project work.
Includes prevention, appraisal, and failure costs.
Auditing processes to ensure quality standards are followed.
Monitoring deliverables to ensure they meet quality requirements.
The degree of uncertainty an organization is willing to accept.
The acceptable variation in project outcomes.
The level at which risk becomes unacceptable.
Avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept, escalate.
Exploit, enhance, share, accept, escalate.
Defines how procurement will be planned, executed, and controlled.
Determines whether work should be performed internally or outsourced.
Includes RFPs, RFQs, and IFBs used to solicit vendor proposals.
Factors used to evaluate vendor proposals, such as cost, experience, and technical capability.
A document that defines team values, agreements, and working norms.
Team development stages: forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning.
Includes Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor, and Expectancy Theory.
Channels = n(n−1)/2, where n = number of stakeholders.
Interactive, push, and pull communication.
Includes listening, empathy, stewardship, and removing impediments.
Sprint planning, daily standup, sprint review, sprint retrospective.
Product backlog, sprint backlog, increment.
Combines predictive oversight with agile flexibility.
A version of a product with just enough features to gather feedback.
Ensuring project outcomes create measurable benefits for stakeholders.
A justification for the project, outlining benefits, costs, and risks.
Describes how and when project benefits will be delivered and measured.
A PMI concept describing areas of focus such as team, development approach, planning, and delivery.
Acting ethically, honestly, and in alignment with PMI's Code of Ethics.
Adapting project management processes to fit the project's size, complexity, and context.
The processes required to unify, coordinate, and manage project activities across all knowledge areas.
The process of defining, preparing, and coordinating all subsidiary plans into a single integrated plan.
Leading and performing the work defined in the project plan to achieve objectives.
Using existing knowledge and creating new knowledge to support project outcomes.
cards.hint: Use what you know; learn what you don't.

cards.definition: Tracking, reviewing, and regulating project progress to meet performance objectives.
Reviewing and approving changes to project baselines.
Finalizing all activities to formally complete the project or phase.
Uncontrolled expansion of project scope without adjustments to time, cost, or resources.
Formal acceptance of completed deliverables by the customer or sponsor.
Monitoring scope and managing changes to the scope baseline.
Planning near-term work in detail and future work at a higher level.
Shortening the schedule without reducing scope using crashing or fast tracking.
Adding resources to shorten schedule duration at increased cost.
Performing activities in parallel that were originally planned sequentially.
cards.hint: Do tasks at the same time.

cards.definition: Estimating individual work packages and rolling them up to total project estimates.
Using historical data from similar projects to estimate duration or cost.
Using statistical relationships (e.g., cost per unit) to estimate project work.
Determining contingency and management reserves for risks.
Summing estimated costs by work package to create the cost baseline.
Specific measures used to evaluate quality performance.
Graphs used to determine whether a process is stable and within control limits.
A visual tool (Ishikawa diagram) used to identify root causes of problems.
A bar chart showing frequency distribution of data.
Shows relationships between two variables to identify trends.
A grid used to prioritize risks based on likelihood and impact.
Assessing risks based on probability, impact, and urgency.
Numerically analyzing the effect of risks on project objectives.
A quantitative technique that simulates thousands of scenarios to predict outcomes.
Assessing the effectiveness of risk responses and processes.
Discussing terms with vendors to reach agreement on scope, cost, and responsibilities.
Completing and settling contracts, resolving claims, and documenting results.
Defines how team members will be acquired, developed, and managed.
The ability to understand and manage your own emotions and those of others.
Common sources include schedules, priorities, resources, technical opinions, and personalities.
A discussion aimed at reaching agreement between parties.
A leadership style focused on supporting the team by removing obstacles and enabling success.
A way of thinking that values collaboration, adaptability, and continuous improvement.
High-level planning that outlines major deliverables and timelines across multiple iterations.
The amount of work a team completes in a sprint, used for forecasting.
A graph showing remaining work over time in a sprint.
Shows work completed over time toward a total scope.
Criteria that a backlog item must meet before being accepted into a sprint.
A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete.
Combining predictive milestones with agile iterations.
High-level needs of the organization that justify the project.
Describe what the system or product must do.
Describe system qualities such as performance, security, and usability.
Identifying the difference between current and desired performance.
Evaluates whether a project is viable technically, financially, and operationally.
Applying PMI's ethical standards to ensure fairness, honesty, and responsibility in project decisions.
A high-level description of the future state the project aims to achieve.
Defines the purpose of the project and why it exists.
The measurable benefit delivered by a project, such as revenue, efficiency, or customer satisfaction.
cards.hint: What the project delivers to the business.

cards.definition: Shared values, beliefs, and behaviors that influence project execution.
Factors considered true for planning purposes but not yet verified.
Limitations such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, and risk.
Any unique and verifiable product, result, or capability produced by the project.
A significant point or event in the project schedule with zero duration.
The amount of time an activity can be delayed without affecting the project end date.
Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the next activity.
Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project finish date.
Specifies working days and hours for the project.
Shows availability of specific resources.
Defines how project costs will be estimated, budgeted, and controlled.
Adjusting the project schedule to align with funding constraints.
An organizational-level statement defining quality expectations.
Comparing project practices or performance to industry standards.
A tool that organizes ideas into categories for analysis.
A structured method for group brainstorming and prioritization.
A technique for evaluating options using weighted criteria.
A hierarchical representation of project risks by category.
Warning signs that a risk is about to occur.
A backup plan used if the primary risk response fails.
Risk that remains after response strategies are implemented.
A new risk created by implementing a risk response.
Describes the work to be performed by a vendor.
Managing and resolving disputes or claims with vendors.
Evaluating team effectiveness and identifying improvement opportunities.
cards.hint: Check how the team is doing.

cards.definition: Includes transformational, transactional, servant, and laissez-faire leadership.
Inspires and motivates the team to exceed expectations.
Focuses on rewards and penalties to manage performance.
A hands-off style where the team has high autonomy.
A leader who prioritizes team needs and removes obstacles.
Agreements on acceptable team behaviors.
Teams that work remotely across locations and time zones.
Determining stakeholder information needs and communication methods.
Information sent to recipients without requiring confirmation.
Information stored for stakeholders to access when needed.
Two-way communication such as meetings or calls.
Product Owner, Scrum Master, Developers.
Includes story points, t-shirt sizing, and planning poker.
A meeting to reflect on the sprint and identify improvements.
A usable product slice delivered at the end of each sprint.
Combines predictive risk planning with agile risk reviews.
Bridges the gap between business needs and project solutions.
Techniques like MoSCoW or Kano to rank requirements.
Assessing whether the delivered solution meets business needs.
Applying honesty, fairness, respect, and responsibility in project decisions.
Includes collaborating, compromising, smoothing, forcing, and withdrawing.
Ongoing effort to improve processes, products, and team performance.
